enrichment.short_description: Open source analytics and monitoring platform that unifies metrics, logs and traces from Prometheus, Loki, Tempo and 150+ other data sources into one dashboard.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (self-hosted OSS or Grafana Cloud free tier)
enrichment.seo_title: Grafana Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Grafana review covering dashboards, Prometheus, Loki and Tempo integration, alerting, pricing tiers and top alternatives for observability teams in 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Grafana is an open source analytics and interactive visualization platform originally created in 2012 by Torkel Odegaard as a fork of Kibana purpose-built for time-series metrics rather than log search. The tool quickly became a standard front end for engineers pairing it with Graphite and later Prometheus, and by 2014 Odegaard partnered with Raj Dutt and Anthony Woods to found the company behind it, initially named Raintank and later rebranded Grafana Labs. The company is nominally headquartered in New York but has operated as a fully remote-first organization since inception, with employees spread across more than 50 countries and no single physical office anchoring the culture. Grafana Labs turned the open source dashboarding tool into the anchor of a broader observability suite that now competes directly with much larger, closed-source monitoring vendors. Grafana Labs has raised roughly 1.09 billion dollars across multiple funding rounds since a 6 million dollar seed round in 2015 led by Lightspeed Venture Partners. That was followed by a 24 million dollar Series A in 2017, a 50 million dollar Series B in 2019, a 220 million dollar Series C in 2020, and a 505 million dollar Series D in 2021 that pushed the company's valuation to 6 billion dollars and made it a unicorn roughly seven years after founding. The company raised a further 270 million dollars in 2024 at the same 6 billion dollar valuation. Notable investors include Sequoia Capital, Coatue, GIC, Lightspeed Venture Partners, Lead Edge Capital and J.P. Morgan. By 2026 Grafana Labs reported surpassing 400 million dollars in annual recurring revenue, serving more than 7,000 organizations including 70 percent of the Fortune 50, with a headcount of roughly 1,800 people. Architecturally, Grafana is a lightweight visualization and alerting layer rather than a data store in its own right: the web application, written primarily in Go and TypeScript/React, connects outward to external systems through a plugin framework covering more than 150 data sources, including Prometheus, InfluxDB, Elasticsearch, MySQL, PostgreSQL, CloudWatch, Azure Monitor and Google Cloud Monitoring. Grafana Labs also builds and maintains the complementary open source projects that make up the so-called LGTM stack: Loki for log aggregation, Grafana for visualization, Tempo for distributed tracing, and Mimir for horizontally scalable, Prometheus-compatible metrics storage. Query languages like PromQL, LogQL and TraceQL let users cross-reference signals, and built-in correlation features let a user jump from a trace span to related logs or metrics without leaving the interface. Core product features include highly customizable dashboards with template variables and more than 30 panel visualization types, a unified alerting engine that routes multi-dimensional alert rules to Slack, PagerDuty or Grafana OnCall, an ad hoc Explore mode for querying data sources directly, Grafana Incident for structured incident response, Synthetic Monitoring for uptime checks, and k6 for load testing, a project Grafana Labs acquired in 2021. Enterprise customers get access to premium data source plugins for tools like Splunk, Datadog and ServiceNow, fine-grained role-based access control, SSO integration, and newer AI-assisted capabilities such as Grafana Machine Learning for anomaly detection and a natural-language assistant for building dashboards and queries. Pricing follows a freemium, usage-based model. The open source core is free to self-host under the AGPLv3 license, which Grafana Labs adopted in 2021 after moving away from Apache 2.0. Grafana Cloud's free tier includes 10,000 active metric series, 50 GB each of logs, traces and profiles ingestion per month, three active visualization users and 14-day retention, with no credit card required. The Pro tier adds a 19 dollar per month platform fee plus usage-based charges of roughly 6.50 dollars per 1,000 active series, about 0.45 dollars per GB of logs, traces or profiles, and 8 dollars per active user. An Advanced tier starts around 55 dollars per month with higher included volumes, and Enterprise plans are custom-priced, typically starting around a 25,000 dollar annual commitment with dedicated support and compliance features. The target audience spans individual developers monitoring home labs up through SRE, DevOps and platform engineering teams at large enterprises running Kubernetes and cloud-native infrastructure. Competitively, Grafana differentiates itself from closed, single-vendor observability suites like Datadog, New Relic and Splunk by embracing a 'big tent' philosophy: bring your own data source rather than being locked into one vendor's collection agents and storage. Compared to Kibana and the broader Elastic stack, Grafana offers stronger native time-series visualization without requiring a business to standardize on Elasticsearch as its only backend, and its usage-based Cloud pricing can be markedly more cost-effective at scale than closed observability platforms, which is a major driver of its adoption among cost-conscious engineering organizations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Grafana began life in 2012 as a Kibana fork built by Torkel Odegaard specifically for visualizing time-series metrics, and it became the company Grafana Labs (originally Raintank) in 2014. Today it is best known as the open source visualization and alerting layer that sits on top of metrics, logs and trace data wherever that data already lives, rather than requiring teams to migrate into a proprietary data store.

The company has grown into a fully remote-first organization with roughly 1,800 employees across more than 50 countries, and it has raised about 1.09 billion dollars in venture funding, reaching unicorn status in 2021 at a 6 billion dollar valuation with backers including Sequoia Capital, Coatue and GIC.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Grafana connects to more than 150 data sources through its plugin architecture, including Prometheus, Loki, Tempo, Elasticsearch and every major cloud provider's native monitoring service, letting teams build unified dashboards that mix metrics, logs and traces in a single pane.

Beyond dashboards, the platform includes unified alerting, an ad hoc Explore query mode, Grafana OnCall for incident escalation, Synthetic Monitoring, k6 load testing, and newer AI-assisted features like anomaly detection and natural-language dashboard generation.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The open source core is free to self-host under the AGPLv3 license. Grafana Cloud adds a managed hosting option starting with a generous free tier covering 10,000 active metric series and 50 GB each of logs, traces and profiles per month with 14-day retention.

Paid Cloud tiers run from a Pro plan at roughly 19 dollars per month plus usage, through an Advanced tier around 55 dollars per month, up to custom Enterprise contracts that typically start near a 25,000 dollar annual commitment.

features.title: Unified Dashboards
features.description: Build dashboards that mix metrics, logs and traces from multiple systems in one visual pane with template variables and 30+ panel types.

features.title: 150+ Data Source Connectors
features.description: Native plugins for Prometheus, Loki, Tempo, Elasticsearch, CloudWatch, Azure Monitor, Google Cloud Monitoring and many more.

features.title: Unified Alerting
features.description: Multi-dimensional alert rules that route notifications to Slack, PagerDuty, email or Grafana OnCall.

features.title: Explore Mode
features.description: Ad hoc, code-free querying across metrics, logs and traces without building a full dashboard first.

features.title: Grafana OnCall
features.description: Incident escalation and on-call scheduling built directly into the Grafana ecosystem.

features.title: Synthetic Monitoring and k6
features.description: Uptime checks and load testing tools, including the k6 project acquired by Grafana Labs in 2021.

features.title: Grafana Machine Learning and Assistant
features.description: AI-driven anomaly detection plus a natural-language assistant for generating dashboards and queries.

features.title: Enterprise Plugins and RBAC
features.description: SSO, fine-grained role-based access control and premium connectors for tools like Splunk and ServiceNow.

pros: Free and open source core with no vendor lock-in to a single data store
Connects to virtually any metrics, logs or trace data source in one interface
Highly customizable dashboards backed by a large community plugin ecosystem
Usage-based Cloud pricing can be more cost-effective at scale than closed observability suites
cons: Usage-based Cloud pricing can become unpredictable for high-cardinality metrics
Self-hosting the full LGTM stack requires meaningful operational expertise
Some advanced features like SSO and premium connectors require Enterprise licensing
PromQL, LogQL and TraceQL have a learning curve for newcomers

faqs.question: Is Grafana free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. The open source core is free to self-host, and Grafana Cloud also offers a free tier with 10,000 metric series and 50 GB of logs, traces and profiles per month.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Grafana and Grafana Cloud?
faqs.answer: Grafana is the self-hosted open source software; Grafana Cloud is Grafana Labs' fully managed hosting service built around the same software plus the LGTM stack.

faqs.question: Does Grafana store data itself?
faqs.answer: No. Grafana is primarily a visualization and alerting layer that queries external data sources like Prometheus, Loki or Elasticsearch rather than owning the underlying data.

faqs.question: What is the LGTM stack?
faqs.answer: LGTM stands for Loki (logs), Grafana (visualization), Tempo (traces) and Mimir (metrics), Grafana Labs' complementary open source observability projects.

faqs.question: Can Grafana replace Datadog or New Relic?
faqs.answer: Many teams use Grafana paired with Prometheus, Loki and Tempo as a lower-cost, vendor-neutral alternative to closed observability suites like Datadog and New Relic.

faqs.question: What license does Grafana use?
faqs.answer: Grafana's core code has been licensed under AGPLv3 since 2021, after previously being licensed under Apache 2.0.

use_cases.title: Infrastructure and Kubernetes Monitoring
use_cases.description: Visualize cluster health, resource usage and application metrics collected by Prometheus and other exporters.

use_cases.title: Business and Executive Reporting Dashboards
use_cases.description: Combine data from SQL databases, APIs and cloud services into shareable business dashboards.

use_cases.title: IoT and Sensor Data Visualization
use_cases.description: Monitor time-series data streaming from industrial sensors, devices and edge deployments.

features: Connect to any supported data source
50 GB logs, traces and profiles per month
~$0.45 per GB of logs, traces and profiles

enrichment.short_description: AI-powered writing assistant that checks grammar, spelling, tone and style in real time across browsers, desktop apps and hundreds of thousands of other apps.
enrichment.seo_title: Grammarly Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Grammarly review covering AI grammar checking, tone detection, plagiarism scanning, pricing plans and top alternatives for writers and teams in 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Grammarly was founded in 2009 by Ukrainian entrepreneurs Alex Shevchenko, Max Lytvyn and Dmytro Lider, building on plagiarism-detection technology the trio had developed years earlier through an earlier venture called MyDropBox. The team rebuilt that foundation into a browser-based grammar and spelling checker aimed squarely at English-language writers, launching the browser extension and web editor that remain the product's core today, with the company eventually headquartered in San Francisco, California. Grammarly operated profitably for years without significant outside capital before raising a 110 million dollar Series A in 2017, followed by 90 million dollars in 2019 and a 200 million dollar round in 2021 that valued the company at 13 billion dollars, backed by investors including General Catalyst, BlackRock, Baillie Gifford, IVP and Spark Capital. In 2025 the company secured an additional 1 billion dollars in growth financing from General Catalyst and expanded well beyond writing assistance, acquiring the productivity and document tool Coda in January 2025 and the Superhuman email client in July 2025, signaling a deliberate repositioning from a single-purpose grammar checker toward a broader AI productivity platform. Technically, Grammarly's engine blends rule-based natural language processing, including part-of-speech tagging and statistical grammar models, with large language models that power its newer generative features. The product is delivered through a wide surface of native integrations rather than a single app: browser extensions for Chrome, Edge, Firefox and Safari, desktop applications for Windows and Mac, a mobile keyboard for iOS and Android, add-ins for Microsoft Office and Google Docs, and a standalone web editor, with the company claiming its assistant can operate inline across roughly 500,000 websites and applications. Core features include real-time grammar, spelling and punctuation correction; a tone detector that flags how a passage is likely to read to its recipient before it is sent; full-sentence rewrite suggestions; a generative AI writing engine, formerly branded GrammarlyGO, that can draft, summarize, reply to or brainstorm text from a prompt; a plagiarism checker that scans submitted text against billions of web pages and academic sources; an AI-generated-text detector; team style guides and brand tone controls; and admin-facing analytics that let organizations track writing quality and product adoption across a workforce. Pricing follows a freemium structure. The Free tier covers basic spelling and grammar correction, tone detection, and 100 AI prompts per month. Grammarly Pro, which folded the former separate Premium and Business plans into a single SKU supporting up to 149 seats, costs about 12 dollars per month when billed annually (or up to 30 dollars per month billed monthly), and adds full-sentence rewrites, plagiarism and AI-content detection, unlimited personalized suggestions and 2,000 AI prompts per member per month. Enterprise plans are custom-priced for larger organizations and layer on single sign-on and SCIM provisioning, audit logs, data loss prevention, bring-your-own-key encryption, custom roles and permissions, and unlimited AI prompts per member. Grammarly's audience spans individual students and professional writers through marketers, customer support and sales teams, and large enterprises standardizing writing quality across thousands of employees. Competitively, its browser-everywhere integration model differentiates it from narrower standalone checkers like ProWritingAid or Hemingway Editor, while its purpose-built, low-friction inline correction workflow differentiates it from general-purpose chat interfaces like ChatGPT that require users to copy and paste text into a separate window. The 2025 acquisitions of Coda and Superhuman, paired with fresh billion-dollar financing, indicate Grammarly is now positioning itself to compete more broadly against AI-native productivity suites rather than remaining solely a grammar and spell-check tool.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Grammarly was founded in 2009 by Alex Shevchenko, Max Lytvyn and Dmytro Lider, evolving from earlier plagiarism-detection work into the browser-based grammar and writing assistant used by hundreds of millions of people today. The company is headquartered in San Francisco and has raised roughly 545 million dollars in disclosed venture funding at a peak valuation around 13 billion dollars, plus a further 1 billion dollar growth round in 2025.

That 2025 round accompanied acquisitions of the productivity tool Coda and the Superhuman email client, marking Grammarly's shift from a single-purpose grammar checker toward a broader AI productivity platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Grammarly combines rule-based grammar and spelling correction with large-language-model-powered generative writing tools, delivered inline through browser extensions, desktop apps, a mobile keyboard, and Microsoft Office and Google Docs integrations covering roughly 500,000 apps and websites.

Beyond basic corrections, it offers tone detection, full-sentence rewrites, a plagiarism and AI-content detector, and team-focused style guides and analytics for organizations standardizing writing quality across staff.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Grammarly's Free tier covers basic grammar and spelling correction plus 100 AI prompts per month. Grammarly Pro, which now replaces the former separate Premium and Business plans, costs about 12 dollars per month billed annually and supports up to 149 seats.
Larger organizations move to a custom-priced Enterprise plan that adds SSO, SCIM, data loss prevention, bring-your-own-key encryption and unlimited AI prompts per member.

features.title: Real-Time Grammar and Spell Check
features.description: Inline correction of grammar, spelling and punctuation as you type across supported apps and websites.

features.title: Tone Detector
features.description: Flags how a message is likely to read to its recipient before it is sent.

features.title: AI Writing Assistant
features.description: Generative AI that can draft, rewrite, summarize or brainstorm text from a prompt.

features.title: Plagiarism and AI-Content Checker
features.description: Scans text against billions of web pages and academic sources and flags likely AI-generated content.

features.title: Full-Sentence Rewrites
features.description: Suggests complete sentence-level rewrites rather than just word-level fixes.

features.title: Cross-Platform Integrations
features.description: Browser extensions, desktop apps for Windows and Mac, a mobile keyboard, and Microsoft Office/Google Docs add-ins.

features.title: Team Style Guides and Brand Tones
features.description: Custom rules and preferred terminology so teams write with a consistent brand voice.

features.title: Admin Analytics
features.description: Reporting for Pro and Enterprise admins to track writing quality and adoption across an organization.

pros: Works inline across hundreds of thousands of websites and applications via browser extension
Free tier is genuinely useful for basic grammar and spelling correction
Tone detection helps avoid miscommunication in professional writing
Strong plagiarism and AI-content detection tools for academic and content use cases
cons: Full feature set, including rewrites and plagiarism checking, requires the paid Pro plan
Monthly AI prompt limits on Free and Pro plans can be restrictive for heavy users
Primarily optimized for English, with limited support for other languages
Browser extension can occasionally conflict with other web apps or affect page performance

faqs.question: Is Grammarly free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Grammarly offers a Free tier with basic grammar and spelling correction and 100 AI prompts per month; advanced features require Pro or Enterprise.

faqs.question: What is Grammarly Pro?
faqs.answer: Grammarly Pro is the current paid plan that replaced the former separate Premium and Business plans, supporting individuals and teams of up to 149 seats.

faqs.question: Does Grammarly work in Google Docs and Microsoft Word?
faqs.answer: Yes, Grammarly offers dedicated add-ins for Google Docs and Microsoft Office alongside its browser extension and desktop apps.

faqs.question: Can Grammarly detect AI-generated text?
faqs.answer: Yes, Grammarly includes an AI-generated-text detector alongside its traditional plagiarism checker.

faqs.question: Is Grammarly safe for confidential documents?
faqs.answer: Grammarly Enterprise offers additional controls like bring-your-own-key encryption and data loss prevention for sensitive content; check the current privacy policy for individual plans.

faqs.question: Who owns Grammarly?
faqs.answer: Grammarly is a private company backed by investors including General Catalyst, BlackRock and Baillie Gifford, last valued around 13 billion dollars.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open source web app for collaboratively browsing, editing and sharing genealogical family tree data, built on the Gramps desktop database engine.
enrichment.pricing_model: Open Source (self-hosted; optional paid third-party hosting)
enrichment.seo_title: Gramps Web Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Gramps Web review covering collaborative family tree editing, self-hosted genealogy hosting, privacy controls, pricing and top alternatives in 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Gramps Web is a browser-based and mobile-friendly companion to Gramps, the long-running open source genealogy desktop application, letting multiple family members research and edit a single shared family tree from any device through a JSON REST API and a Progressive Web App front end. The wider Gramps project traces back to April 21, 2001, when Don Allingham shared a personal hobby project he had built as a GEDCOM editor to research his own father's genealogy; his father reportedly suggested the name 'Gramps,' which the community later reverse-engineered into the backronym Genealogical Research and Analysis Management Programming System. The first stable Gramps desktop release arrived on February 11, 2004, after several years of gestation, and the desktop application remains actively developed more than two decades later. Gramps Web itself emerged considerably later as a separate sub-project, developed primarily by contributor David Straub, who began public work on a browser-based front end (initially referred to as gramps-webapp) around 2020 before it matured into the dedicated Gramps Web project with its own site at grampsweb.org and its own GitHub organization repository. Gramps Web has no company or venture funding behind it. It is maintained entirely as a community open source effort under the umbrella of the broader Gramps Project, and both the web app and its REST API are licensed under the GNU Affero General Public License version 3, which requires that any hosted, modified version of the software also make its source code available to users, a meaningfully stronger copyleft position than the desktop application's older licensing. Technically, Gramps Web pairs a Python-based REST API backend with a Progressive Web App frontend, and it is designed for full interoperability with the existing Gramps data model: family trees can move bidirectionally between the desktop application and the web app using the Gramps Web Sync add-on, and the software supports Gramps XML and GEDCOM import and export so users are never locked into the platform. Deployment is Docker-first, with Docker Compose being the recommended and most convenient self-hosting path; images are published for both standard x86-64 servers and ARM architectures, meaning the entire stack can run on a low-cost device like a Raspberry Pi, and a DigitalOcean one-click app is also available for users who prefer a managed cloud VM without a full custom setup. Core features include interactive ancestor, descendant, fan and hourglass family tree chart visualizations; place-mapping tools that plot family history events and residences on interactive maps, including support for custom historical map overlays; record-level privacy controls that let an owner mark data private and control exactly which collaborators can see it, with private records filtered out at the database query layer rather than merely hidden in the interface, for stronger protection of sensitive information about living relatives; the ability to generate most of the same PDF reports the Gramps desktop app supports, including relationship graphs and book-style reports, directly from the browser; a lightweight blogging feature for turning research into narrative family history stories with embedded photos; full data export so users can download their tree data, media files and user accounts at any time for backup or migration to a different server; and a user interface translated by the community into roughly 40 languages. Because the underlying software is entirely free, the only recurring cost most users face is hosting. Users comfortable with Docker can self-host at effectively no cost beyond their own server, while those who prefer a managed option can pay a third-party provider such as Grampshub, run by one of the Gramps Web project's core contributors, to handle infrastructure, backups and updates. This free, self-hostable, data-portable model makes Gramps Web particularly attractive to genealogy hobbyists, extended families and local history societies who want an Ancestry.com- or MyHeritage-style collaborative research experience without recurring subscription fees or being locked into a proprietary data format, trading away the automated historical-record hint matching those commercial services offer in exchange for full data ownership, strong privacy controls and deep interoperability with the mature, two-decade-old Gramps desktop ecosystem.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Gramps Web is the collaborative, browser-based counterpart to Gramps, an open source genealogy desktop application that Don Allingham started as a personal hobby project in 2001. Gramps Web itself was developed later, led primarily by contributor David Straub, emerging as a public project around 2020.

It has no company or investors behind it and is maintained as a community open source project licensed under the AGPL-3.0, which requires hosted derivatives of the software to also share their source code.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Gramps Web offers interactive ancestor, descendant, fan and hourglass family tree charts, historical map overlays, and record-level privacy controls that filter private data at the database layer so sensitive information about living relatives stays protected.

It syncs bidirectionally with the Gramps desktop app, generates the same PDF reports the desktop version supports, and lets users export all of their data at any time, avoiding vendor lock-in entirely.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The software itself is completely free and open source. Users who are comfortable with Docker can self-host at no software cost, including on low-cost hardware like a Raspberry Pi.
Those who prefer not to manage a server can pay a third-party host such as Grampshub, run by a core Gramps Web contributor, for managed hosting, backups and updates.

features.title: Interactive Family Tree Charts
features.description: Explore ancestor, descendant, fan and hourglass chart visualizations of a family tree.

features.title: Collaborative Multi-User Editing
features.description: Multiple family members can research and edit a shared tree from any browser or device.

features.title: Historical Map Overlays
features.description: Plot family history events and places on interactive maps with custom historical overlays.

features.title: Privacy-Aware Record Permissions
features.description: Private records are filtered out at the database layer, not just hidden in the UI, for stronger data protection.

features.title: Gramps Desktop Sync
features.description: Bidirectional synchronization with the Gramps desktop app via the Gramps Web Sync add-on.

features.title: Full Data Export
features.description: Download all tree data, media files and user accounts at any time for backup or migration.

features.title: In-Browser Report Generation
features.description: Generate PDF relationship graphs and book-style reports without leaving the browser.

features.title: Self-Hosted Docker Deployment
features.description: Runs via Docker Compose on standard servers or ARM devices like a Raspberry Pi.

pros: Completely free and open source with no required subscription
Full interoperability with the mature, two-decade-old Gramps desktop application
Strong, database-level privacy controls, well suited to sensitive living-relative data
Self-hostable, giving users complete ownership of their genealogical data
cons: Requires comfort with Docker or a paid host like Grampshub for non-technical users
Lacks the automated historical record hints and document matching of commercial services like Ancestry
Smaller community and slower feature development pace than venture-backed competitors
No dedicated native mobile app; relies on the mobile web experience

faqs.question: Is Gramps Web free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Gramps Web is free and open source under the AGPL-3.0 license; the only potential cost is hosting if you choose a managed provider.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Gramps and Gramps Web?
faqs.answer: Gramps is the original desktop genealogy application; Gramps Web is a browser-based, multi-user collaborative front end built on the same underlying data model.

faqs.question: Can I sync Gramps Web with the Gramps desktop app?
faqs.answer: Yes, using the Gramps Web Sync add-on, data can be synchronized bidirectionally between the desktop app and Gramps Web.

faqs.question: Does Gramps Web support automated record hints like Ancestry?
faqs.answer: No, it is a family tree management and collaboration tool rather than a historical-record matching service.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Gramps Web?
faqs.answer: Yes, the recommended method is Docker Compose, and it also supports ARM devices such as a Raspberry Pi.

faqs.question: What license is Gramps Web released under?
faqs.answer: Gramps Web and its API are licensed under the GNU Affero General Public License version 3 (AGPL-3.0).

use_cases.title: Collaborative Family History Research
use_cases.description: Let extended family members contribute to and edit a shared family tree together.

use_cases.title: Genealogy Society Record Keeping
use_cases.description: Maintain and share community genealogical records with controlled access.

use_cases.title: Privacy-Conscious Family Tree Hosting
use_cases.description: Keep sensitive data about living relatives fully under the family's control.

features: Unlimited users and trees limited only by server resources
Backups and updates handled by the host

enrichment.short_description: Open source, headless e-commerce platform built on .NET Core and MongoDB, supporting B2B, B2C, multi-store, multi-vendor and multi-tenant storefronts.
enrichment.pricing_model: Open Source (self-hosted; paid partner support and services)
enrichment.seo_title: GrandNode Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: GrandNode review covering the open source .NET and MongoDB e-commerce platform, multi-vendor features, headless architecture, pricing and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: GrandNode is an open source e-commerce platform built on ASP.NET Core with MongoDB as its primary data store rather than a traditional SQL database, positioning it as a developer-first alternative to platforms like Magento or Shopify for merchants who want full source-code control and a composable, headless architecture. The project started in 2015 when a small team of developers set out to build what they describe as the first e-commerce platform based on .NET and MongoDB, releasing an initial beta in December 2015 and the first stable version in January 2016. The codebase shares conceptual roots with the popular ASP.NET platform nopCommerce, and GrandNode documents a migration path for moving data out of nopCommerce's SQL schema into GrandNode's MongoDB structure, but it evolved into an independently maintained project with its own architecture rather than remaining a simple fork. The team behind GrandNode is based in Krakow, Poland. GrandNode has no publicly disclosed venture capital funding. Instead, the project sustains itself through a certified partner and reseller program, which launched with 29 initial partners and reached its first platinum-tier partner by the end of 2019, along with paid customization, implementation and support services sold by that partner network. The core platform itself remains free and open source, with the source code published on GitHub under the grandnode organization. On the technical side, the current major version, GrandNode 2, runs on .NET Core 5 and above with MongoDB, AWS DocumentDB or Azure CosmosDB as supported data stores, and it ships a Vue.js-based administration panel alongside a plugin architecture for extending checkout flows, shipping rules, tax calculation and payment processing. The platform can be containerized with Docker and deployed on AWS, Azure or self-managed infrastructure, with support for S3-compatible object storage for product media and assets, and it is explicitly designed for horizontal scalability given MongoDB's document-oriented, sharding-friendly nature compared to a relational database. Core commerce features include native multi-store support for running several storefronts from a single installation, multi-vendor marketplace functionality for platforms hosting third-party sellers, multi-tenant capability for SaaS-style deployments serving many independent merchants, and built-in multi-language and multi-currency support without needing third-party extensions. GrandNode also supports both B2B and B2C selling models in the same platform, including customer-specific pricing and quote-style workflows for wholesale buyers alongside a standard consumer checkout, and its headless, API-first design allows the built-in storefront to be replaced entirely with a custom React, Vue or Next.js front end while keeping the .NET backend for catalog, order and inventory management. Additional built-in tooling covers SEO-friendly URLs and metadata, discount and promotion engines, and marketplace vendor commission management for multi-vendor deployments. Pricing follows a pure open source model: the core platform is free to download, self-host and modify under its published open source license, with no subscription fee for the software itself. GrandNode's revenue comes from its network of certified partner agencies, which offer paid implementation, hosting and ongoing support services, plus any premium plugins or themes sold through the partner ecosystem, rather than from a SaaS-style recurring license fee charged directly by GrandNode. This structure makes GrandNode particularly attractive to .NET development shops and digital agencies building custom or marketplace storefronts for clients who find Magento too resource-heavy or find PHP-based platforms like WooCommerce and PrestaShop a poor architectural fit for teams already standardized on the Microsoft development stack. Competitively, GrandNode occupies a smaller but distinct niche within the open source e-commerce landscape, positioned alongside platforms like Saleor, Medusa and its spiritual relative nopCommerce, differentiated primarily by its MongoDB-based data layer, native multi-vendor and multi-tenant capabilities, and headless-first architecture aimed at development teams rather than non-technical merchants looking for a point-and-click storefront builder.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: GrandNode is an open source e-commerce platform built on ASP.NET Core and MongoDB, started in 2015 by a small team based in Krakow, Poland, who set out to build the first e-commerce platform combining .NET with a MongoDB data layer. The first stable release shipped in January 2016.

The project shares conceptual roots with the ASP.NET platform nopCommerce and documents a migration path from nopCommerce's SQL schema, but it has evolved into an independently maintained platform with its own multi-store, multi-vendor and headless architecture.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: GrandNode natively supports multi-store, multi-vendor marketplace and multi-tenant deployments from a single installation, along with built-in multi-language and multi-currency support, without requiring third-party extensions.

Its headless, API-first design lets developers swap the default storefront for a custom React, Vue or Next.js front end while keeping the .NET backend for catalog, order and inventory management, making it well suited to custom commerce builds.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The core GrandNode platform is entirely free to download, self-host and modify, with the source code published openly on GitHub.
Revenue for the project comes from a certified partner network offering paid implementation, hosting and support services rather than from a direct subscription fee for the software.

features.title: Multi-Store and Multi-Tenant Architecture
features.description: Run several storefronts or independent merchant tenants from a single GrandNode installation.

features.title: Multi-Vendor Marketplace Support
features.description: Built-in tools for hosting and managing third-party sellers on a marketplace storefront.

features.title: B2B and B2C Commerce in One Platform
features.description: Supports customer-specific wholesale pricing alongside standard consumer checkout flows.

features.title: Headless, API-First Storefront
features.description: Swap the default storefront for a custom React, Vue or Next.js front end while keeping the .NET backend.

features.title: Multi-Language and Multi-Currency
features.description: Native support for serving multiple languages and currencies without extra plugins.

features.title: MongoDB-Based Data Layer
features.description: Document-oriented storage designed for horizontal scalability compared to relational databases.

features.title: Built-In SEO and Promotion Tools
features.description: SEO-friendly URLs, metadata controls and discount and promotion engines out of the box.

features.title: Docker-Based Deployment
features.description: Containerized deployment on AWS, Azure or self-managed infrastructure with S3-compatible storage support.

pros: Free and fully open source with complete access to the source code
Native multi-vendor and multi-tenant support without extra plugins
Built on .NET Core and MongoDB, appealing to teams already in the Microsoft stack
Headless architecture allows a fully custom storefront front end
cons: Smaller theme and plugin ecosystem than Magento or Shopify
Requires .NET and MongoDB expertise to self-host and maintain
Documentation and community support are thinner than larger open source platforms
No official managed cloud hosting directly from GrandNode; relies on partner agencies

faqs.question: Is GrandNode free?
faqs.answer: Yes, GrandNode is free and open source software that you can self-host and modify.

faqs.question: What database does GrandNode use?
faqs.answer: GrandNode uses MongoDB by default, with support for AWS DocumentDB and Azure CosmosDB as alternatives.

faqs.question: Can GrandNode support a marketplace with multiple vendors?
faqs.answer: Yes, multi-vendor marketplace functionality is built into the platform.

faqs.question: Is GrandNode headless?
faqs.answer: Yes, it offers an API-first design that allows the default storefront to be replaced with a custom frontend.

faqs.question: Who maintains GrandNode?
faqs.answer: GrandNode is maintained by a small development team based in Krakow, Poland, supported by a network of certified partner agencies.

faqs.question: Does GrandNode offer hosting?
faqs.answer: GrandNode itself does not sell managed hosting directly; certified partners offer hosting, implementation and support services.

use_cases.description: Serve wholesale buyers with customer-specific pricing and quote workflows.

use_cases.description: Pair the GrandNode backend with a custom React or Vue storefront.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open source drag-and-drop web builder framework and SDK for building custom email, landing page and website editors without hardcoding templates.
enrichment.pricing_model: Open Source core plus Freemium SDK
enrichment.starting_price: Free (open source core and free Studio SDK tier)
enrichment.seo_title: GrapesJS Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: GrapesJS review covering the open source drag-and-drop web builder framework, the Studio SDK, pricing tiers and top no-code alternatives in 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: GrapesJS is an open source, framework-agnostic JavaScript library for building drag-and-drop visual editors that output clean HTML and CSS. Rather than being consumed as a standalone end-user product, it is most commonly embedded inside other software: email marketing tools, CMS platforms, e-commerce page builders and internal admin tools that need a template editor. It was created by Italian developer Artur Arseniev and first released in 2016 after he ran into a recurring frustration at his day job, where newsletter templates built for clients had to be hardcoded by hand every single time a client requested a change. Arseniev built a reusable drag-and-drop template editor to solve that problem and open-sourced it rather than keeping it proprietary. Nearly a decade later, the project has grown to more than 25,800 GitHub stars and over 4,600 forks, and according to its creators it now sits underneath hundreds of production SaaS products spanning email marketing, CMS, e-commerce, e-learning and internal tooling categories. In 2025, Arseniev co-founded a new company, Grapes Studio Inc, alongside Griffin Kelly, a Y Combinator alumnus and serial entrepreneur, to commercialize a hosted, AI-powered version of the editor aimed at challenging WordPress-style website builders. Grapes Studio is backed by Open Core Ventures, the venture studio founded by GitLab co-founder Sid Sijbrandij that specifically invests in and supports companies built around a popular open source project using an open-core business model. Grapes Studio launched publicly out of stealth in October 2025, positioning itself as an AI-powered, HTML-first alternative to WordPress and other legacy website builders. Importantly, the underlying GrapesJS open source project itself remains freely licensed and community maintained on GitHub under the GrapesJS organization, independent of the new commercial entity. Architecturally, the core GrapesJS library is a component-based editor engine that can be used standalone or wrapped for React, Vue or Angular applications, rendering a live drag-and-drop canvas alongside a style manager for visual CSS editing, a layer manager for navigating the component tree, and a code export view for viewing or exporting the generated HTML and CSS. The library ships a plugin architecture with official presets, including one specifically tuned for email-safe, table-based HTML that renders correctly across major email clients, and a preset for general webpage building. On top of the core library, GrapesJS Studio provides a commercial layer: the @grapesjs/studio-sdk package on npm packages the editor as an embeddable, white-labelable component designed for SaaS products, alongside a hosted GrapesJS Studio web application for building editor projects directly in the browser. Core features include the drag-and-drop visual canvas with a nested component tree; a code-free style manager for adjusting CSS visually; import and export of raw HTML and CSS or structured component JSON; email-safe HTML rendering presets; a plugin and command API for adding custom blocks and behaviors; multi-project and multi-domain support within the Studio SDK for teams managing several embedded editor instances; and, in the newer Studio product, AI-assisted content generation for building pages and templates directly from natural-language prompts. Pricing follows a two-tier model. The core GrapesJS library remains free and open source for developers who want to self-host it and integrate it directly into their own codebase, with no licensing fee. The commercial Studio SDK layers on a metered, session-based pricing model: a Free tier covering 1,000 sessions per month on a single domain with Studio branding, a Startup tier at 200 dollars per month for 20,000 sessions and custom branding, a Business tier at 2,000 dollars per month for 50,000 sessions with wildcard domain support and dedicated Slack access, and a custom-priced Enterprise tier offering unlimited sessions, 24/7 support and custom development. This dual-track model lets GrapesJS serve two distinct audiences simultaneously: individual developers and agencies who want a free, self-hosted editor framework to build into their own products, and SaaS companies that would rather pay for a ready-made, brandable page-builder component than engineer one from scratch in-house. Competitively, the open source core positions GrapesJS as a free alternative to proprietary embeddable editor SDKs, while the newer Grapes Studio product, still very early in its commercial life, positions the broader GrapesJS ecosystem as an emerging AI-powered challenger to WordPress and other mainstream website and email builders.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: GrapesJS is an open source, framework-agnostic drag-and-drop editor framework created by Artur Arseniev in 2016 to solve the problem of hardcoding email newsletter templates by hand for every client change. It is most commonly embedded inside other software products rather than used as a standalone application.

In 2025 Arseniev co-founded Grapes Studio Inc with Y Combinator alumnus Griffin Kelly, backed by Open Core Ventures, to build a commercial, AI-powered version of the editor, while the underlying GrapesJS open source project remains freely available on GitHub.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The core library offers a drag-and-drop canvas, a visual style manager, and a plugin architecture with presets for email-safe HTML and general webpage building, usable standalone or wrapped for React, Vue or Angular.

The commercial Studio SDK packages the editor as an embeddable, white-labelable component for SaaS products, with multi-project support and, in the hosted Studio app, AI-assisted template generation from prompts.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The GrapesJS core library is completely free and open source, with no license fee for self-hosting and integrating it into your own codebase.

The Studio SDK adds paid, session-based tiers starting with a free plan for 1,000 sessions per month, scaling through Startup at 200 dollars per month and Business at 2,000 dollars per month, up to custom Enterprise pricing.

pros: Core framework is free, open source and fully self-hostable
Framework-agnostic, making it easy to embed inside React, Vue or Angular apps
Large, mature plugin ecosystem after nearly a decade of development
Studio SDK lets SaaS teams add a page builder without building one from scratch
cons: Raw GrapesJS requires developer effort to integrate and style into a production-ready editor
Studio SDK paid tiers can get expensive quickly based on session volume
Free open source tier has fewer dedicated support resources than paid SDK customers
The newer AI and hosted Studio features are still early and less battle-tested than the core library

faqs.question: Is GrapesJS free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the core GrapesJS library is free and open source; the commercial Studio SDK adds paid tiers for embeddable, white-labeled use.

faqs.question: What is the GrapesJS Studio SDK?
faqs.answer: It is a paid, packaged version of the GrapesJS editor designed to be embedded directly inside SaaS products.

faqs.question: What is Grapes Studio?
faqs.answer: Grapes Studio is a separate hosted, AI-powered website and email builder company founded by GrapesJS creator Artur Arseniev in 2025.

faqs.question: Can GrapesJS build email templates?
faqs.answer: Yes, it includes presets that generate email-safe, table-based HTML compatible with major email clients.

faqs.question: Does GrapesJS require React or Vue?
faqs.answer: No, it is framework-agnostic and works standalone or wrapped for popular frontend frameworks.

faqs.question: Who created GrapesJS?
faqs.answer: GrapesJS was created by Italian developer Artur Arseniev, who first released it in 2016.

enrichment.short_description: Open source routing engine and commercial Directions API providing turn-by-turn navigation, route optimization, isochrones and map matching on OpenStreetMap data.
enrichment.seo_title: GraphHopper Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: GraphHopper review covering the open source routing engine, Directions API, route optimization pricing tiers and top alternatives for developers in 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: GraphHopper is a fast, memory-efficient routing engine built on OpenStreetMap data, available both as a free open source Java library or standalone server and as a commercial hosted Directions API offering routing, matrix, isochrone, map matching and route optimization endpoints for cars, trucks, bicycles and pedestrians. The open source GraphHopper project began in 2014, and in January 2016 its developers, together with the team behind the vehicle-routing optimization library jsprit, formally incorporated GraphHopper GmbH in Munich, Germany, to commercialize the underlying technology as a hosted API and to offer enterprise support and licensing to businesses. Company leadership includes Peter Karich, Stefan Schroder and Michael Zilske. GraphHopper GmbH remains an independent, bootstrapped German company rather than a venture-backed startup. Public sources describe a small team of around four employees working remotely from different cities and countries, and there is no public record of institutional funding rounds; the business is instead sustained by paid API subscriptions and enterprise licensing built on top of its two open source projects, which together have accumulated over 6,000 GitHub stars. On the technical side, the core routing engine is released under the Apache License 2.0 and written in Java, and it can run either embedded as a library inside another Java application or as a standalone web server. It builds a routing graph directly from OpenStreetMap data and applies contraction hierarchies and other speedup techniques to calculate routes quickly even across continent-scale road networks. The engine supports configurable vehicle profiles for cars, trucks, bikes and pedestrians, plus a 'custom models' feature that lets developers tune routing costs, for example to avoid tolls or unpaved roads, entirely through configuration rather than by forking and modifying the underlying code. The commercial Directions API bundles several distinct endpoints built on that same engine: a Routing API for point-to-point and turn-by-turn navigation, a Matrix API for calculating many-to-many travel times and distances at once, an Isochrone API for generating reachability polygons showing how far someone can travel within a given time, and a Map Matching API for snapping noisy GPS trace data onto the actual road network. A separately priced Route Optimization API, built on the jsprit vehicle-routing-problem solver, targets last-mile delivery and fleet dispatch use cases where multiple vehicles need efficient multi-stop routes. All of these APIs share the same underlying OpenStreetMap-based routing graph and offer global coverage. Pricing follows a freemium, credit-based model. The Free tier provides 500 credits per day but is capped at five locations per request and restricted to non-commercial use only. Paid tiers scale up from there: Basic at 69 euros per month (5,000 daily credits, up to 30 locations and 2 vehicles per request), Standard at 199 euros per month (15,000 daily credits, up to 80 locations and 10 vehicles), and Premium at 479 euros per month (50,000 daily credits, up to 200 locations and 20 vehicles, with phone support). A custom Enterprise tier offers bulk discount pricing for very high-volume needs, supporting up to 10,000 locations or 200 vehicles per request with dedicated support. All paid tiers include a white-label option and email support with SLA guarantees. This pricing structure, combined with the option to self-host the open source engine entirely for free, positions GraphHopper as a notably lower-cost, open-source-friendly alternative to Google Maps Platform, Mapbox and HERE Technologies for developers who need routing, isochrone and fleet optimization capabilities without the pricing complexity or vendor lock-in of the larger commercial mapping platforms. It is particularly popular among logistics, last-mile delivery, mobility and field-service companies that value both cost predictability and the ability to fall back on self-hosting the engine if they ever need to.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: GraphHopper is a routing engine built on OpenStreetMap data, available as a free open source Java library or as a commercial hosted Directions API. The open source project began in 2014, and GraphHopper GmbH was incorporated in Munich, Germany in January 2016 to commercialize it.
The company remains small and independent, reportedly around four employees working remotely, with no public venture funding, sustained instead by paid API subscriptions and enterprise licensing.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The Directions API bundles a Routing API, a Matrix API for many-to-many travel calculations, an Isochrone API for reachability polygons, and a Map Matching API for snapping GPS traces to roads, all built on the same OpenStreetMap-based graph.

A separately priced Route Optimization API, built on the jsprit vehicle-routing solver, targets last-mile delivery and fleet dispatch, while custom vehicle profiles let developers tune routing for trucks and specialized vehicles.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: A Free tier offers 500 credits per day for non-commercial use, capped at five locations per request. Paid tiers scale from Basic at 69 euros per month through Standard at 199 euros and Premium at 479 euros per month.
A custom Enterprise tier supports up to 10,000 locations or 200 vehicles per request with dedicated support, and the underlying open source engine can also be self-hosted entirely for free.

pros: Open source core can be self-hosted for free with no per-call fees
Significantly cheaper than Google Maps Platform or Mapbox at scale
Covers routing, matrix, isochrone, map matching and optimization in one API family
Fine-grained custom routing profiles for trucks and specialized vehicles
cons: Free tier is limited to non-commercial use and low request volume
Small company size, reportedly around four employees, means limited support bandwidth
Routing quality depends on OpenStreetMap data coverage, which can vary by region
Self-hosting the engine requires Java and infrastructure expertise

faqs.question: Is GraphHopper free?
faqs.answer: The open source routing engine is free to self-host, and the hosted Directions API offers a free tier for non-commercial use with 500 credits per day.

faqs.question: What is the difference between GraphHopper open source and the Directions API?
faqs.answer: The open source project is a self-hosted Java library or server; the Directions API is GraphHopper GmbH's fully hosted commercial service built on the same engine.

faqs.question: Does GraphHopper support truck routing?
faqs.answer: Yes, through custom vehicle profiles that account for truck-specific restrictions like height, weight and toll avoidance.

faqs.question: What is the Route Optimization API?
faqs.answer: A separately priced endpoint built on the jsprit solver for planning efficient multi-stop routes across a fleet of vehicles.

faqs.question: What map data does GraphHopper use?
faqs.answer: GraphHopper builds its routing graphs from OpenStreetMap data.

faqs.question: Who owns GraphHopper?
faqs.answer: GraphHopper is owned by the independently held GraphHopper GmbH, headquartered in Munich, Germany.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open source graph visualization software that renders DOT-language graph descriptions into diagrams for networks, hierarchies and structured data.
enrichment.seo_title: Graphviz Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Graphviz review covering the open source DOT-language graph visualization tool, layout engines, output formats, licensing and top alternatives in 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Graphviz, short for Graph Visualization Software, is a mature open source toolkit for turning text-based graph descriptions into diagrams. It is used across software engineering, networking, bioinformatics, database design and machine learning wherever relationships between entities need to be visualized automatically rather than drawn by hand. The project originated at AT and T Bell Labs, with the earliest documented work by researchers Eleftherios Koutsofios and Stephen North described in a technical report published in September 1991. It grew out of AT and T's need for automatic layout tools capable of visualizing large telecommunications network graphs and software call-graph structures, and the resulting 'dot' hierarchical layout algorithm, along with its accompanying DOT description language, became the project's signature and most widely used contribution to the field of graph drawing. Graphviz has no company or investors behind it. The software remains owned by AT and T, through the lineage of AT and T Labs Research and the former Lucent Bell Labs, but it has been released as free and open source software, currently under the Eclipse Public License. It is maintained largely by a small group of volunteer contributors who coordinate development on GitLab rather than by a commercial entity with a product roadmap or sales team. Technically, Graphviz ships as a set of command-line tools and C libraries that parse graphs written in the DOT language, a simple declarative syntax for describing nodes, edges, clusters and their attributes, and then render those graphs using one of several specialized layout engines. The 'dot' engine handles hierarchical, layered directed graphs such as flowcharts and dependency trees; 'neato' and 'fdp' produce force-directed, spring-model layouts suited to undirected graphs; 'sfdp' scales force-directed layout to very large graphs; 'circo' arranges nodes in circular layouts, often used for cyclic structures; and 'twopi' produces radial layouts. Additional tools such as 'osage' and 'patchwork' provide clustered and treemap-style layouts for specialized use cases. Core features include support for more than 40 output formats, spanning raster images like PNG, JPEG and GIF, vector formats like SVG, PDF, PostScript and EPS, and structured or interactive formats including JSON, image maps and VRML. Users have extensive control over node and edge styling, including custom colors, fonts, line styles, embedded hyperlinks, and HTML-like tabular node labels for representing structured data within a single node. Because the toolkit is implemented as libraries and command-line binaries rather than a locked-down application, developers have built bindings and wrappers in numerous programming languages, including Python, Java, Perl, Ruby and JavaScript, the latter through libraries like viz.js, letting Graphviz generate diagrams programmatically from data structures such as abstract syntax trees, software dependency graphs, state machines and organizational charts. Because Graphviz is fully free and open source, it has no pricing tiers, subscriptions or commercial edition of any kind; there is nothing to purchase and no vendor relationship to manage. In practice, Graphviz is typically consumed as an embedded dependency inside other developer tools rather than used directly as a standalone end-user application: documentation generators like Doxygen and Sphinx, database schema visualization tools, continuous integration pipelines that render dependency graphs, and countless smaller developer utilities all rely on Graphviz under the hood to produce diagrams. Its competitive position rests less on matching the visual polish or interactivity of modern JavaScript-based visualization libraries such as D3.js, Mermaid or Cytoscape, and more on being an extremely lightweight, dependency-free, fully scriptable rendering engine that has remained a de facto standard for generating static graph diagrams in technical documentation and automated build pipelines for well over three decades.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Graphviz originated at AT and T Bell Labs, with the earliest documented work published in a September 1991 technical report by researchers Eleftherios Koutsofios and Stephen North. It grew out of AT and T's need to automatically lay out large telecommunications network and software call graphs.

The software has no company or investors behind it; it remains owned by AT and T but has been released as free and open source software under the Eclipse Public License, maintained largely by volunteer contributors.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Graphviz parses graphs written in the DOT language and renders them using specialized layout engines, including 'dot' for hierarchical diagrams, 'neato' and 'fdp' for force-directed layouts, and 'circo' and 'twopi' for circular and radial layouts.
It supports more than 40 output formats, from raster images and SVG to PDF and structured JSON, along with rich node and edge styling and language bindings for Python, Java, JavaScript and more.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Graphviz is entirely free and open source software with no pricing tiers, subscriptions or commercial edition.
It is most commonly consumed as an embedded dependency inside other developer tools, such as documentation generators and CI/CD pipelines, rather than purchased or licensed directly.

features.title: DOT Language Graph Description
features.description: A simple declarative text syntax for describing nodes, edges, clusters and attributes.

features.title: Multiple Layout Engines
features.description: Includes dot, neato, fdp, sfdp, circo and twopi for hierarchical, force-directed, circular and radial layouts.

features.title: 40+ Output Formats
features.description: Export to SVG, PDF, PNG, JSON and many other raster, vector and structured formats.

features.title: Custom Node and Edge Styling
features.description: Control colors, fonts, line styles, hyperlinks and HTML-like tabular node labels.

features.title: Command-Line Tools and C Library
features.description: Use Graphviz as standalone binaries or embed its rendering library in another application.

features.title: Language Bindings
features.description: Bindings and wrappers for Python, Java, Perl, Ruby and JavaScript, among others.

features.title: Clustered and Radial Layouts
features.description: Specialized layout tools like osage and patchwork for treemap and clustered structures.

features.title: Embeddable in Documentation Pipelines
features.description: Commonly used inside tools like Doxygen, Sphinx and CI/CD dependency graph renderers.

pros: Completely free, open source and dependency-light
Extremely mature and stable after more than three decades of development
Wide output format support makes it easy to embed rendered diagrams anywhere
Fully scriptable and automatable, ideal for generating diagrams programmatically from code
cons: Default visual styling looks dated compared to modern interactive visualization tools
No built-in interactivity or drag-and-drop diagram editing by default
Learning the DOT language and layout engine behavior takes time for complex graphs
Large or dense graphs can produce cluttered layouts without careful manual tuning

faqs.question: Is Graphviz free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Graphviz is fully open source and free to use, with no paid tiers.

faqs.question: What is the DOT language?
faqs.answer: DOT is the declarative text format Graphviz uses to describe a graph's nodes, edges and attributes.

faqs.question: What file formats can Graphviz export to?
faqs.answer: Graphviz supports more than 40 output formats, including SVG, PDF, PNG and JSON.

faqs.question: Does Graphviz have a graphical editor?
faqs.answer: No, Graphviz is primarily a command-line and library-based rendering engine rather than a GUI editor.

faqs.question: Who maintains Graphviz today?
faqs.answer: It is maintained largely by volunteer contributors, while ownership of the software remains with AT and T.

faqs.question: Can I use Graphviz from Python or JavaScript?
faqs.answer: Yes, community bindings exist for Python, Java, Ruby and JavaScript, among other languages.

enrichment.short_description: Free, open source flat-file PHP CMS with no database requirement, offering Git-friendly Markdown content, a modern admin panel and an extensive plugin ecosystem.
enrichment.pricing_model: Open Source (free core; paid premium themes and plugins)
enrichment.seo_title: Grav Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Grav review covering the open source flat-file PHP CMS, admin panel, plugin ecosystem, premium themes pricing and top alternatives in 2026.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Grav is an open source content management system built in PHP that stores all content and configuration as plain text and Markdown files instead of relying on a database, aiming to combine the simplicity and portability of a static site generator with the dynamic, on-demand rendering and convenient admin interface of a traditional CMS like WordPress. Grav was created by Andy Miller, a co-founder of the Joomla project and founder of the premium theme studio RocketTheme, who set out to build a free, open source flat-file CMS with all the polish of a commercial product but without any licensing cost to end users. The project was first released in 2014 and reached its 1.0 stable milestone on November 12, 2015, after roughly a year and a half of open development, with RocketTheme serving as its first, and for many years primary, premium theme provider. Grav has no outside venture investors. It is developed and commercially supported by Trilby Media LLC, Andy Miller's company and the successor entity behind RocketTheme, which monetizes the free core CMS by selling premium themes, plugins and support bundles directly on the Grav website rather than through a subscription SaaS model. The underlying software itself remains permanently free and is released under the permissive MIT License. On the technical side, Grav 2.x is built on a modern PHP 8.3 and above foundation using the Symfony 7 framework and Twig 3 templating engine. Content lives as folders of Markdown files with YAML frontmatter, which can be version-controlled directly in Git and copied between servers without any database export or migration step, making a Grav site fully portable by design. The platform ships a first-party REST API that powers both its Admin 2.0 single-page-application interface, built with SvelteKit 5, Vite and Tailwind CSS for near-instant page navigation, a customizable dashboard and real-time collaborative editing, and headless integrations for developers who want to consume Grav's content from a separate front end. A newer addition is a Model Context Protocol server that lets AI agents interact with the CMS using the same permission model and API surface available to human administrators. Core features beyond the flat-file architecture include the Grav Package Manager, referred to as GPM, and a built-in command-line console for installing and updating themes and plugins without touching the file system manually; a Debug Bar for development diagnostics; built-in multi-language support for internationalized sites; an automatic image optimization pipeline that handles resizing, cropping and format conversion; full-text search, with a paid 'YetiSearch Pro' upgrade available for production-scale search needs; and an extensive plugin and theme marketplace covering use cases from blogs and portfolios to documentation sites. Pricing follows a classic open-core model. The Grav core CMS, its Admin 2.0 panel and the GPM package manager are entirely free to download and self-host, with no limit on the number of sites a user can run. Revenue for Trilby Media comes from optional premium products sold on top of that free core, such as individual premium themes, for example the Helios theme priced around 50 dollars, and larger bundled packages combining documentation-focused themes, editors and search tooling priced around 200 dollars, each sold with lifetime updates and support included. This structure makes Grav attractive to developers and agencies who want WordPress-like editing convenience without the operational overhead of installing, securing and migrating a MySQL database, and it is often chosen specifically for its Git-native workflow. Competitively, Grav positions itself as a developer-centric middle ground between traditional database-backed content management systems like WordPress and Joomla on one side, and pure static site generators like Jekyll and Hugo on the other: it offers dynamic, on-demand page rendering and a polished admin interface, but with none of the database management burden that comes with the larger, more established CMS platforms.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Grav is an open source flat-file CMS built in PHP that stores content as Markdown files rather than in a database. It was created by Andy Miller, a Joomla co-founder and RocketTheme founder, first released in 2014 and reaching its 1.0 stable milestone in November 2015.
Grav has no outside investors; it is developed and commercially supported by Trilby Media LLC, which sells premium themes and plugins on top of a core CMS that remains free under the MIT License.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Grav 2.x runs on PHP 8.3+, Symfony 7 and Twig 3, storing content as Git-friendly Markdown files with YAML frontmatter, fully portable without any database migration.

It ships the Admin 2.0 SvelteKit-based dashboard with real-time collaborative editing, the Grav Package Manager and CLI console, built-in multi-language support, automatic image optimization, and even a Model Context Protocol server for AI agent access.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The Grav core CMS, admin panel and package manager are completely free to download, self-host and use on unlimited sites under the MIT License.
Trilby Media generates revenue through optional premium themes, such as the Helios theme around 50 dollars, and bundled packages around 200 dollars that include lifetime updates and support.

features.title: Flat-File, Database-Free Architecture
features.description: Content and configuration are stored as plain text and Markdown files instead of a database.

features.title: Git-Friendly Markdown Content
features.description: Version control all content and configuration directly in Git using Markdown files with YAML frontmatter.

features.title: Admin 2.0 Dashboard
features.description: A SvelteKit-based single-page admin interface with real-time collaborative editing.

features.title: Grav Package Manager and CLI Console
features.description: Install and update themes and plugins from the command line without manual file management.

features.title: First-Party REST API and MCP Server
features.description: Headless content access plus a Model Context Protocol server for AI agent integration.

features.title: Built-In Multi-Language Support
features.description: Native internationalization for building multi-language sites.

features.title: Automatic Image Optimization
features.description: A pipeline that handles resizing, cropping and format conversion automatically.

features.title: Extensive Plugin and Theme Marketplace
features.description: A wide range of free and premium plugins and themes for blogs, portfolios and documentation sites.

pros: Completely free, open source core under the permissive MIT License
No database to install, secure or migrate; content is portable plain-text files
Native Git version control for all content and configuration
Modern, fast admin interface with real-time collaborative editing
cons: Smaller plugin and theme ecosystem than WordPress
Flat-file architecture can be less efficient than a database for very large sites
Premium themes and advanced search require additional paid purchases
Requires more technical comfort with Git, the CLI and YAML than typical point-and-click site builders

faqs.question: Is Grav free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Grav core CMS is free and open source under the MIT License.

faqs.question: Does Grav use a database?
faqs.answer: No, Grav is a flat-file CMS that stores content as Markdown files with YAML frontmatter instead of a database.

faqs.question: Can I version control a Grav site with Git?
faqs.answer: Yes, Grav is designed natively for Git-based workflows since all content lives as plain text files.

faqs.question: Who created Grav?
faqs.answer: Grav was created by Andy Miller, a Joomla co-founder and founder of the theme studio RocketTheme.

faqs.question: Does Grav have an admin panel?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Admin 2.0 single-page application dashboard provides a full graphical editing interface.

faqs.question: Are there premium Grav themes?
faqs.answer: Yes, Trilby Media sells premium themes and plugin bundles, with individual themes starting around 50 dollars.

use_cases.title: Developer Blogs and Documentation Sites
use_cases.description: Build fast, Git-versioned technical blogs and documentation without a database.

use_cases.title: Agency Client Websites Needing Git-Based Workflows
use_cases.description: Deliver client sites with version-controlled content and simple portability between servers.

use_cases.title: Lightweight Portfolio and Brochure Sites
use_cases.description: Run small, fast-loading marketing or portfolio sites without database overhead.
